integration"}]},{"id":"step-09","depends_on":[],"step_type":"galactic_potential_model","summary":"Adopt MWPotential2014 from galpy (spheroidal bulge, Miyamoto-Nagai disk, NFW used for integration"}]}],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":181,"context":"MWPotential2014 as default Also integrate in Law & Majewski 2010 potential for comparison.","inputs":["Galactocentric positions and velocities","potential"],"outputs":["orbital parameters (eccentricity, apocenter, pericenter)"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":181,"end_line":182,"context":"integrate orbit forward and backward 1.5 Gyr"}]},{"id":"step-11","depends_on":["step-10"],"step_type":"escape_or_bound_assessment","summary":"Determine if star is bound or unbound based on orbit integration. The HVS is found to be unbound in MWPotential2014, reaching >700 kpc and no bound orbit.","inputs":["orbital parameters"],"outputs":["bound status"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":390,"end_line":391,"context":"HVS is completely unbound from the Milky Way"}]},{"id":"step-12","depends_on":["step-10","step-11"],"step_type":"origin_assessment","summary":"Discuss possible origins of HVS, ruling out black hole ejection and suggesting dynamical interactions or systematic proper motion errors.","inputs":["orbit","bound status"],"outputs":["origin hypothesis"],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":392,"end_line":403,"context":"possible origins of the HVS candidate"}]}],"candidates":[],"candidate_groups_considered":[{"group_id":"chem-candidates","description":"68 stars selected by k-means clustering as Sgr chemical candidates, with 62 having Gaia proper motions. The HVS identifier is not available in the provided source files (only top of Table 1 shown; full catalog external).","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":292,"end_line":298,"context":"orbit group classification including HVS"},{"kind":"text","path":"literature/1901.04559/arxiv_source/trace_sgr_stream_resub.tex","start_line":304,"end_line":325,"context":"Table 1 header and note indicating full list external"}]},{"group_id":"hvs-candidate","description":"The hypervelocity star candidate identified as unbound from the Milky Way, with total Galactocentric velocity 577 km/s. message.reasoning_content:
We are asked to perform STAGE 1: SCAFFOLD AND ROSTER. We need to fill in extraction (status, summary), full method_chain, candidate_groups_considered, and for candidates, only identifiers stubs for objects that the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way. No other candidate fields yet. The paper: "Identifying Sagittarius Stream Stars By Their APOGEE Chemical Abundance Signatures". It mentions a hypervelocity star candidate (HVS) as possibly unbound. The review says "论文提及发现一颗超高速星候选体（hypervelocity star candidate）." So we have one HVS candidate. Also check if any other objects are possibly unbound? The paper describes a group "HVS (1 star)" that is unbound. Are there any other candidates? The text says: "We also find one hypervelocity star candidate" (line 134). In the results (section 4.4), they describe the HVS, total Galactocentric space velocity 577 km/s, orbit integration suggests it's unbound. They mention that it's completely unbound from the Milky Way. Also they note that it's the only one. So there is one candidate. The paper does not present any other unbound candidates. So the roster will contain exactly one candidate. However, we need to check if there are any other stars that might be considered possibly unbound? The "accreted halo" stars are bound, high eccentricity but bound. Bulge stars are bound. Sgr stream stars are likely bound. So only the HVS. So extraction status: candidates_found? Yes, one candidate. But we need to fill extraction.summary accordingly. Extraction confidence: we'll need to assess later, but for now just status. The method_chain must be constructed from the paper's description. We need to list inputs (APOGEE DR14, Gaia DR2, etc.), then steps: cross-match, quality cuts, distance estimation, radial velocities from APOGEE, solar position and motion assumptions, velocity calculation, orbit integration in MWPotential2014, escape/bound assessment, origin assessment. The candidate origin assessment for the HVS is discussed. The method chain includes the clustering analysis for chemical tagging that led to identifying the candidates, then kinematic analysis with orbits. That's part of candidate selection, not strictly bound/unbound assessment? The paper uses chemical clustering to identify Sgr stream candidates, then uses Gaia proper motions and distances to compute orbits and assess membership. For the HVS, they note that its orbit is unbound. So method_chain: step-01: input_catalog (APOGEE DR14, Sgr core sample), step-02: sample_selection (quality cuts, stellar parameter cuts), step-03: cross_match (Gaia DR2 via TOPCAT), step-04: distance_estimation (photometric distances from APOGEE VAC), step-05: radial_velocity_measurement (APOGEE), step-06: solar_position_and_motion (R0=8.0 kpc, v_circ=220 km/s, solar motion from Schönrich2010), step-07: velocity_calculation (compute UVW, Galactocentric velocities), step-08: galactic_potential_model (MWPotential2014), step-09: orbit_integration (forward/backward 1.5 Gyr), step-10: escape_or_bound_assessment (check if unbound), step-11: origin_assessment (discuss possible ejection scenarios). Also chemical clustering is part of sample selection: k-means clustering to identify chemical candidates, but that's for selection of the Sgr-like sample, not directly for unbound status. But the HVS was selected from that sample, so we should include that selection step. The paper uses kinematic control sample to tune clustering. So method_chain should include: input_catalog (APOGEE DR14), sample_selection (apply S/N, T_eff, [Fe/H] limits, no ASPCAPBAD flag), then cross_match with Gaia. But also the clustering step could be considered a "sample_selection" for chemically similar stars. But we need to capture the essential steps that lead to the bound/unbound assessment. I'll structure the method_chain as:

step-01: input_catalog (APOGEE DR14 and Sgr core sample)
step-02: sample_selection (apply quality cuts to select MW stars similar to Sgr core in stellar parameters: 3550-4200 K, S/N>70, no ASPCAPBAD flag, [Fe/H]>-1.2)
step-03: chemical_clustering (k-means in 6D chemical space to identify Sgr-like stars; tune k=16) - step_type could be candidate_classification? Or maybe sample_selection? I think it's a selection step. We have step_type: sample_selection? Or we could use "candidate_classification"? There's a step_type "candidate_classification". That seems appropriate. So step-03: candidate_classification (k-means clustering selects Sgr stream chemical candidates). Depends on step-02.
step-04: cross_match (Gaia DR2 proper motions via TOPCAT nearest neighbor within 1") depends on step-03.
step-05: distance_estimation (photometric distances from APOGEE VAC, Hayden 2014 method) - but note that distances were used in the selection? The clustering did not use distances, but later kinematic analysis uses distances. So we have distance estimation as a step, but it might have been done before orbit integration. The paper says: "Distances to the APOGEE stars are derived by methods described in ... and are accurate to ~20%." So distances are obtained from the VAC. That could be step-05, depends on step-02 (or step-01). Then radial_velocity from APOGEE: step-06 radial_velocity_measurement (APOGEE DR14). Then solar_position_and_motion step-07. velocity_calculation step-08: from proper motions, distance, RV, convert to Galactocentric UVW and total velocity. Then galactic_potential_model step-09 (MWPotential2014). orbit_integration step-10. escape_or_bound_assessment step-11. origin_assessment step-12 (optional). So method_chain steps.

We need to include solar_position_and_motion step with parameters: R0, v_circ, solar motion U/V/W. The paper states: "we assume a circular velocity of 220 km s^-1 at a Solar radius of 8.0 kpc from the Galactic center, and the Solar motion as measured by Schönrich2010." So we need to extract the Schönrich2010 solar motion values? The paper doesn't list them explicitly, just cites it. Schönrich et al. 2010 gives U_sun = 11.1 km/s (positive outward), V_sun = 12.24 km/s (in direction of Galactic rotation), W_sun = 7.25 km/s (toward NGP). But we need to record the values as given in the paper, but only if the paper states them. The paper does not state the numbers, only references. So we should put parameters with raw_value: "Schönrich2010" or something, but the skill says: "If the paper does not state them, keep the step and say in summary that the solar parameters are not reported; do not guess values." So we'll have a step with summary that says solar parameters are adopted from Schönrich+2010 but not explicitly listed. We can still include parameters with name solar_motion_u, etc., but values unknown? The schema expects raw_value and value for MethodParameterRecord. We could set raw_value and value to the citation, but not the numeric values. The requirement says: "Record the paper's stated values (R0, z0, circular velocity at the Sun, solar peculiar motion U/V/W) as structured parameters[] entries with raw_value, cleaned value, unit, and source_refs. If the paper does not state them, keep the step and say in summary that the solar parameters are not reported; do not guess values." So for R0=8.0 kpc, v_circ=220 km/s, those are stated. For solar motion, the paper does not state numbers. So we can have parameters for R0 (8.0 kpc) and v_circ_sun (220 km/s). For solar motion U, V, W, we can't fill numeric values; perhaps we can note "adopted from Schönrich+2010" but not the numbers. But the schema requires raw_value and value. Value should be a string. We could put "adopted from Schönrich+2010" as raw_value, and empty value? The schema requires both raw_value and value; value must be a string. So we might set value empty? But it says "required". Hmm, MethodParameterRecord has required: name, raw_value, value. So we must provide something. If the paper doesn't state the numbers, we might set raw_value = "assumed from Schönrich+2010" and value = ""? That would violate required. We'll need to put a placeholder maybe "not_stated" but that's not a number. The instructions: "If the paper does not state them, keep the step and say in summary that the solar parameters are not reported; do not guess values." So we can still include the parameter entries but with raw_value and value as "not reported" or something? But the schema expects any string. So we can put raw_value: "from Schönrich+2010", value: ""? But required non-empty? The schema definition for MethodParameterRecord: "raw_value": {"title": "Raw Value", "type": "string"}, "value": {"title": "Value", "type": "string"}. Both required. It doesn't say they can't be empty strings. So we can set value: "" and raw_value: "adopted from Schönrich+2010". That might pass validation? The validator might complain about empty string. Possibly better to set raw_value: "not reported" and value: "not reported". I'll do raw_value: "not explicitly stated; assumed from Schönrich+2010", value: "". But to be safe, I'll put a note. I'll include parameters for solar_motion_u, solar_motion_v, solar_motion_w with raw_value "not reported (see Schönrich+2010)" and value "". However, the validator might want a numeric value for these but they are not numeric. It's okay as long as they are strings. Also we need to include source_refs for those parameters.

Now candidate_groups_considered: We need to list groups that were considered but not included as candidates. The paper's Table 1 lists the Sgr Stream Cluster with membership categories: a, b, c, d, e. The HVS is d. So the group is the whole table of 62 stars (or 68 candidates without Gaia). But only one is HVS candidate. The other stars are bound (Sgr stream, accreted halo, bulge, others). So we need to document that the chemical candidate sample was reviewed but only the HVS is possibly unbound. So candidate_groups_considered should include a group for the chemically selected sample. For example: group_id: "chem-selected-sample", description: "68 stars selected by k-means clustering as Sgr chemical candidates, of which 62 have Gaia proper motions. The paper identifies one HVS candidate among them.", decision: "partially_included", reason: "Only the HVS candidate is treated as possibly unbound; the rest are bound (Sgr stream, accreted halo, bulge, others)." source_refs: from text lines discussing the HVS and classification. Also there might be other groups like the kinematic control sample, but those are not candidates for unbound. So we'll include that group. Additionally, the full APOGEE sample is not a candidate group for unbound, but we can note that we considered only the chemical candidates. It might be sufficient to have one group. The skill says: "Use candidate_groups_considered[] to record the tables or object groups that were reviewed and why they were excluded." For a single candidate, we need to include at least one group showing that the rest were excluded. So I'll create a group for the "Sgr chemical candidate sample", and note that only one star is HVS, others are bound. Also maybe another group for the full APOGEE sample? Not necessary.

Now the roster: one candidate. Identifiers: paper_candidate_id: The APOGEE ID of the HVS. From Table 1, we have a sample of memberships, but the full table is not printed; only first few rows are shown, and the HVS is not shown? The table captions says "The full list is available from the online journal." So we don't have the APOGEE ID for the HVS from the provided source. The excerpt table starts with 2M001... but only shows a, a, etc. The paper does not explicitly state the APOGEE ID of the HVS in the main text? Let's check the paper text. In Section 4.4, they describe the HVS: "One star in the Sgr chemical sample is at a Galactocentric distance of ~54 kpc... This star ... has a total Galactocentric space velocity of ~577^{+209}_{-129} km/s ... If this hypervelocity star's orbit does not trace back to the Galactic center...". But they don't give the APOGEE ID. The only place to get the ID would be the full table, which is not provided in our source files. The catalog_review.json notes that the paper mentions a hypervelocity star candidate, but no internal tables or external resources. The catalog_extraction.json summary: table_count=0. So we do not have the full candidate table. The skeleton has ecsv_paths empty. So we cannot identify the specific APOGEE ID for the HVS from the given files. So what to do? The skill says: "If only a subset of the paper's candidates is individually identifiable in these files (e.g. a printed top-N table while the full catalog lives in an external data file), the roster is that identifiable subset and the inaccessible remainder must be documented in candidate_groups_considered." So we have no identifiable candidate because we lack the full table that lists the HVS's ID. So our roster would be empty? But the paper clearly states there is one HVS candidate, but its identifier is not explicitly given in the provided text. We could use the paper's description, but no APOGEE ID. We could perhaps infer from the text? The table header has APOGEE ID column, and we can see sample rows, but the HVS is likely not among those shown. So we cannot fill a paper_candidate_id. Thus we must document this as an inaccessible candidate. The extraction status then should be "partial" or "needs_review"? The skill says: "If only a subset of the paper's candidates is individually identifiable in these files (e.g. a printed top-N table while the full catalog lives in an external data file), the roster is that identifiable subset and the inaccessible remainder must be documented in candidate_groups_considered." So we can include a candidate group for the HVS but note that its identifier is not available in the provided files. However, we still need to provide identifiers for any candidates we can. In this case, we cannot provide any identifier. So the candidates array would be empty. But extraction status should be "partial" because we know there is a candidate but cannot extract its identifiers. However, instructions say "Return ONLY the JSON document, minified". I think we should set status: "partial", summary: "One HVS candidate identified but no identifiers available in source files; full catalog table is external.", and candidate_groups_considered to include the HVS with reason "identifier not available". But also include the group of chemical candidates to show exclusion. The roster (candidates array) would be empty. Then method_chain we can still fill because we know the steps.

Alternatively, we could use the APOGEE ID "HVS candidate" as the paper_candidate_id? The schema requires a paper_candidate_id string, but if unknown we can't fabricate one. So we should not guess. So empty candidates list.

But the skill says "Return an EXHAUSTIVE roster of identifier stubs: one entry per object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way". Since we have zero identifiable, we return empty array. That's acceptable. And we document in candidate_groups_considered that the HVS candidate could not be identified.

Let's construct the JSON accordingly.

step-11: id: "step-11", step_type: "escape_or_bound_assessment", summary: "Determine if star is bound or unbound based on orbit integration. The HVS is found to be unbound in MWPotential2014, reaching >700 kpc and no bound orbit.", depends_on: ["step-10"], inputs: ["orbital parameters"], outputs: ["bound status"]. source_refs: lines 390-391.
